coredata.dc:title: Controlling the emissive pathways of carbon nanoparticles by selective surface functionalization
coredata.dc:creator: Al Farsi, Basim
Sumesh Sofin, R.G.
El-Shafey, El-Said I.
Issac, Abey
Al Marzouqi, Faisal
Al Mashaikhi, Said
Zar Myint, M.T.
Abou-Zied, Osama K.
coredata.dc:description: Environmental friendly and biocompatible fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) show great potential for various applications. The functional groups on the surface of the NPs dramatically influence the absorption and emission characteristics of the particles. However, a conclusive emission mechanism of CNPs and the exact role of functional groups are still lacking. Herein, a systematic investigation has been carried out by quantifying the oxygen-based function groups (–OH, –CHO, –COOH, –CO, –COO) present on the surface of activated carbon (AC) nanoparticles to identify their role in the emission characteristics. AC samples, prepared by the activation process of green waste of mandarin peels, were subjected to further treatments to achieve different levels of surface functionality with oxygen containing functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, phenol and lactone. Various characterization techniques were employed to identify and quantify the functional groups. A detailed emission study revealed the role of specific functional groups in the emission process. Our results suggests that various emissive pathways in CNPs can be controlled by selective surface functionalization. This study shows significant results, which could shed more light on the emission mechanism of CNPs and the pivotal role of surface functional groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
Activated carbon
Oxidized activated carbon
Deconvoluted PL spectrum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AC and OAC samples for characterizations
Structure and morphology of AC samples:

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: His Majesty's Trust Fund for Strategic Research
His Majesty’s Trust Fund for Strategic Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Sultan Qaboos University within the framework of research Internal Grant [IG/SCI/PHYS/18/05] and His Majesty's Trust Fund for Strategic Research (SR/SCI/CHEM/18/01). Further, we thank the central analytical and applied research unit at Sultan Qaboos University for the XRD measurements. Mr. Abdul Rahman Al Nabhani, Department of pathology, Sultan Qaboos University is acknowledged for the TEM measurements and Prof. Salim Al-Harthi for XPS measurements.

We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Sultan Qaboos University within the framework of research Internal Grant [IG/SCI/PHYS/18/05] and His Majesty’s Trust Fund for Strategic Research (SR/SCI/CHEM/18/01). Further, we thank the central analytical and applied research unit at Sultan Qaboos University for the XRD measurements. Mr. Abdul Rahman Al Nabhani, Department of pathology, Sultan Qaboos University is acknowledged for the TEM measurements and Prof. Salim Al-Harthi for XPS measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the surface functionalization of activated carbon nanoparticles (AC).
(a–d) TEM images of (a) AC, (b) OAC-OH, (c) OAC-Am, and (d) OAC-HN; (e–h) the distribution of particle size; (i-l) lattice spacing of AC, OAC-OH, OAC-Am, and OAC-HN, respectively.
(a) Surface functionality, (b) pHzpc, (c) effect of pH on zeta potential, (d) FTIR spectra of the nanoparticles of the AC and OAC samples.
(a) TGA and (b) DSC of AC and OAC nanoparticles.
Deconvolution of C 1s and O 1s peaks: (a,e) AC, (b,f) OAC-OH, (c,g) OAC-Am, and (d,h) OAC-HN.
Normalized absorption spectra of AC and OAC samples in ethanol.
Room-temperature (a) emission spectra, (b-e) deconvoluted spectra, and (f) integrated emission intensity (Area %) of the AC and OAC samples in ethanol.

Schematic presentation of the possible excitation and emission pathways from different states associated with various oxygen based functional groups on the surface of AC and OAC-HN (sample with highest SF). NR represents nonradiative transitions.
(a) Emission transients (310 nm excitation) of AC and OAC samples. (b) Total lifetime of the samples. (c) Amplitudes of short and long lifetime components.
EDS and CHN analysis of the AC samples.
Atomic content of carbon hybridization and oxygen groups in AC and OAC obtained by XPS.

head.ce:title: Controlling the emissive pathways of carbon nanoparticles by selective surface functionalization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental friendly and biocompatible fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) show great potential for various applications. The functional groups on the surface of the NPs dramatically influence the absorption and emission characteristics of the particles. However, a conclusive emission mechanism of CNPs and the exact role of functional groups are still lacking. Herein, a systematic investigation has been carried out by quantifying the oxygen-based function groups (–OH, –CHO, –COOH, –CO, –COO) present on the surface of activated carbon (AC) nanoparticles to identify their role in the emission characteristics. AC samples, prepared by the activation process of green waste of mandarin peels, were subjected to further treatments to achieve different levels of surface functionality with oxygen containing functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, phenol and lactone. Various characterization techniques were employed to identify and quantify the functional groups. A detailed emission study revealed the role of specific functional groups in the emission process. Our results suggests that various emissive pathways in CNPs can be controlled by selective surface functionalization. This study shows significant results, which could shed more light on the emission mechanism of CNPs and the pivotal role of surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) have attracted remarkable consideration due to their interesting physical and chemical properties which are applicable to various fields such as bio-imaging, sensor applications, medical diagnostics, etc. [1–4]. The main classification of FNPs is based on the presence and absence of heavy metal elements in the NP structure [5,6]. Some metal oxide and heavy metal sulfide NPs are well-known fluorescent materials such as CdO, CdS, CdSe, and CdTe [7–10]. However, applications of these particles are limited by the toxicity of the metals such as Cd, Se, and Te. Therefore, researchers are deeply focusing on metal-free organic FNPs due to their environment friendliness [11–13]. Among all, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have attracted wide attention due to their exceptional properties. They form a stable aqueous suspension [14] and have ionic stability [15]. Emission of CNPs is in the visible region [16] and shows up-conversion photoluminescence (UPL)[17]. In addition, they are pH-sensitive [18] and have an excited-state lifetime of a few nanoseconds [19]. Their synthesis is easy and cost-effective [20]. CNPs are biocompatible and environmentally-friendly [21,22].

The emission mechanism of CNPs is still under discussion [23,24]. However, some of the proposed mechanisms have gained wide acceptance as a reasonable model for emission. In one of the models, the emission is attributed to four main mechanisms: the band gap transitions corresponding to the presence of sp2-nanodomains, the surface-defects/traps, the molecular state determined by the fluorescent molecules connected on the surface or in the interior of the CNPs, and crosslink enhanced emission effect [25,26]. Another well-cited model suggests that photo-excitation only occurs at the core of the CNP, and from there radiative transitions to the ground level occurs [23,24]. A different study shows that isolated sp2 domains attached to the surface of the CNPs can have a significantly higher energy gap compared to the core gap and plays a prominent role in the emission process [27]. It has been suggested that the photo-excited electrons can relax or be trapped in emissive surface/trap states due to defects and surface functional groups. Radiative transitions to the ground state are possible from these states as well [23,24]. In all these reported models, the importance of surface states in the emission mechanism is emphasized. There are studies on the role of various functional groups in the emission mechanism. For example the surface states created by groups such as pyridine derivatives, –OH,–CO, –COOH and their role in the emission mechanism through charge transfer are reported [27,28]. However, a conclusive emission mechanism for CNPs is still lacking.

The oxygen containing functional groups (–OH, –CHO, –COOH, –CO, –COO) showed a strong potential in modifying the optical properties of CNPs [29]. These functional groups directly create various emission states and non-radiative states. They could also form an electron trapping state to compete with the radiative emission states [29]. Geethalakshmi et al. employed state-of-the-art theoretical simulations to evaluate the OH functionalization effect on the ionization potentials, light absorption, and emission properties. The result suggested that the dependence of emission on the functionalization sites and large electron-hole separation offers a strategy to tune the optical gap and photoluminescence properties [30]. Li et al. found that functional groups containing C = O double bond (−CHO, −COCH3, and –COOH) are more advantageous compared to other groups in tuning the optical and electronic properties due to its small charge transfer character and large frontier orbital hybridization [31]. Steady-state optical studies on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with abundant functional groups show that the PL centers may locate at the edge sites and defects, including the hybrid structures formed by oxygen-containing functional groups on the edges or the flat surface [32,33]. Feng et al. used time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) to study the effect of five types of oxygen-containing functional groups (–COOH, –COC–, –OH, –CHO, and –OCH3) on the emission of GQDs. The results showed that oxygen containing groups located on the surface have more evident effects on the optical properties than those on the edge of the GQD plane [34]. It has been reported that, like GQDs, traditional CNPs with sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon core or sp2 domains embedded in amorphous carbon also have various functional groups on the surface (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic groups) which takes part in the emission process [1,2,33,35–39]. The occurrence of functional groups is strongly dependent on the synthesis methods.

CNPs can be synthesized by numerous methods [38] such as laser ablation [15,39] electrochemical oxidation [22,40] arc-discharge [41], hydrothermal synthesis [37,42,43] microwave-assisted synthesis [44,45] ultrasound-based synthesis [46] chemical methods and carbon activation process [47–49]. The latter showed remarkable results in providing a very rich functionalized surface. The functional groups on the NP surface include carboxyl, lactone, phenol, and carbonyl. These functional groups on the surface of CNPs depend on the precursor materials, preparation conditions, and activation methods. The formation of a rich functionalized surface can be utilized for further surface modification to have the desired amount of selected functional groups [48,50].

To the best of our knowledge, there are no systematic quantitative investigations on the effect of different oxygen-based functional groups on the emission characteristics of CNPs. Therefore, in this work, activated carbon (AC) NPs were prepared by the activation of green waste of orange mandarin peels. Surface modification of the NPs was achieved chemically by surface acidification using nitric acid (HNO3), persulfate ((NH4)2 SO8), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The structure and morphology of the NPs, along with the quantification of the different functional groups on their surface, were obtained via multiple characterization techniques. Detailed photoluminescence studies were performed in order to determine the role of specific functional groups in the emission characteristics of the NPs. The results are quite imperative for understanding the emission mechanism of CNPs.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Mandarin peels were collected from the local market and dried under sunlight. The dry peels were washed with deionized water to remove all surface impurities. Then, it was left to dry again at room temperature. The clean dried peels were cut into small pieces (1–2 cm) and used for the preparation of carbon particles.

AC was synthesized by NaOH activation method [51]. Carbonization was achieved by placing dry and clean mandarin peels in the heated zone of a horizontal tube furnace (GSL-1100X-110 V, MTI Corporation, USA). The peels were pyrolyzed at 700 °C for 2 h under nitrogen (N2) atmosphere. After the carbonization step, 40 g of the produced black char was impregnated with a saturated solution of NaOH (120 g of concentrated sodium hydroxide in 600 mL of deionized water), in a weight ratio of NaOH to char of 3:1. The mixture heated in an oven (Hobersal Mon XB2-125 furnace, Hobersal, Spain) at 130 °C overnight to carbonize. In order to achieve the activation, the dry sample (without washing) was heated for 2 h in a horizontal tube furnace at 700 °C. After heating, the sample (AC) was washed thoroughly with deionized (DI) water until alkalinity was almost removed. To keep the acidic functional groups on the carbon in a protonated form, AC was washed several times with 0.1 M hydrochloric acids followed by DI water until no acidity was detected by methyl orange indicator in the rinse water after cleaning. Then AC was dried at 105 °C for 24 h in an oven until no weight change was detected in the sample due to water evaporation. The Activated carbon was stored in a dry and clean polyethylene bottle for further characterization.

The activated carbon after drying at 105 °C in the oven for 24 h was crushed to powder form. Then, 0.2 g of this powder was dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol and centrifuged for 5 min. The upper part of the solution containing unsettled fine particles of AC was removed by a pipette and dried. These particles were used for the preparation of oxidized activated carbon (OAC) samples by acidic treatments. Acidic treatment was carried out to modify the density of functional groups on the surface of AC. OAC was prepared by mixing AC with three different chemicals; nitric acid, hydrogen peroxide, and ammonium persulfate separately. This resulted in three different set of samples having different density of oxygen based functional groups on the surface of AC NPs. The first set (OAC-NH) was prepared by mixing 20 g of AC with concentrated nitric acid in the ratio 1:6 (g: mL), and by heating at 80 °C for 6 h. OAC-NH was then left in warm deionized water for 48 h to remove excess acid. Thereafter it was washed several times and filtered under vacuum. The washing continued until no acidity was detected by methyl orange indicator in the rinse water. Finally, OAC-NH was dried in the oven at 105 °C for one day until no weight change was detected in the sample due to water evaporation. Second set of samples (OAC-OH) was prepared by mixing 16 g of AC with 96 mL of hydrogen peroxide in the ratio 1:6. The mixture was kept under agitation in a dark place for 2 days to complete the reaction. After that, samples were thoroughly washed and allowed to dry at 105 °C. In order to prepare the third set (OAC-Am), 46 g of ammonium persulfate was added to diluted sulfuric acid to form a saturated solution and then 16 g of AC was dissolved in it. The mixture was left for 48 h under agitation. OAC-Am was then washed and dried. A schematic representation of surface functionalization of AC NPs are given in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AC and OAC samples for characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The AC and OAC particles (prepared by the methods explained in the previous sections) were subjected to further purification process to extract the nanosized particles for characterizations. In general, 10 g of AC/OAC powder was added to 100 mL of DI water and sonicated for 30 min. Then the suspension was left to settle down. The top layer, still consisting of nanosized and unsettled particles, was collected. This top layer of the suspension was centrifuged to extract the nanoparticles, which was subsequently dried. This process was repeated until a desirable amount of pure AC/OAC nanosized powder was obtained. A schematic presentation is provided to summarize the separation process of carbon nanoparticles in Fig. S1. These nanosized particles were used for further characterizations in the form of powder, pellets and dispersion in ethanol or water as described in the subsequent sections.

The structural characterization of the samples were done using X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Philips diffractometer, model PW 1820.) with Cu- Kα incident beam at wavelength λ = 1.5406 Å. To study the morphology and lattice structure, the samples were examined using transmission electron microscope (TEM, model JEM-1400-JEOL). Surface morphology and microstructure of the samples were studied by using a field emission electron microscope (FESEM, Jeol JSM-7800F). The elemental analysis was done by using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). A multi-probe X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Omicron Nanotechnology, Germany) with (Al) Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) radiated at 15 kV was used for the surface characterization. The XPS results were analyzed using Casa XPS software (Casa Software Ltd, UK). The infrared absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum One, USA) with scanning rage between 4000 and 400 cm−1; the signal resolution was 4 cm−1 with 40 scans. Samples in pellet form were used for the FT-IR measurements. To make the pellets, 0.005 g of NPs was mixed with 0.995 g of KBr, and the mixture was pressed under high pressure to form compact pellets. The elemental analysis was carried out using a CHNS/O analyzer (Euro EA 3000 elemental analyzer, UK). Zeta potential was measured as a function of pH using SurPASSTM electrokinetic analyzer (UK). During the experiment, the solution's pH was adjusted with 0.01 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl. Photoluminescence measurements were carried out using Shimadzu RF–5301 spectrometer. Transient lifetime measurements were done by using a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (TimeMaster, Photon Technology International). Zero point of charge (ZPC) of the carbon particles was estimated by following the procedure reported by Moreno-Castilla et al. (2000) [51]. 0.1 g of carbon particles mixed with 20 mL of CO2-free water. The flask was sealed with a cap and left with occasional agitation for 48 h at room temperature. The pH of the filtrate was measured using a calibrated pH meter and the measured value is the point of zero charge. Leyva-Ramos et al. and El-Shafey et al. have used this method successfully [51–53].

Boehm titrations method was employed to quantify the functional groups on the surface of CNPs using selective base neutralization capacity [54]. 0.125 g of each AC samples were mixed with 25 mL standard solutions of 0.1 M NaOH, 0.1 M NaHCO3, 0.1 M NaOEt, and 0.05 M Na2CO3 in 30 mL polyethylene vials. N2 gas was flushed through each sample for 5 min to remove oxygen. This was done to prevent the probability of base-catalyzed auto-oxidation of carbon in the presence of oxygen [55]. The samples were left in a shaking water bath for 48 h at 25 °C (100 rpm) to reach equilibrium. The fall in each solution's concentration was quantified by titrating all aliquots against standardized 0.1 M HCl, applying methyl orange as an indicator. Indeed, the amount of surface functionality calculated by the Boehm titration is significantly affected by dissolved CO2 in the solution, so it must be absolutely removed to ensure reliable and precise results. Since it has been observed that removing CO2 from the solutions used in a direct titration (as is frequently done for the NaOH reaction base) is extremely difficult, a back-titration for each of the reaction bases NaHCO3, Na2CO3, and NaOH should be performed. To ensure adequate CO2 elimination, the acidified aliquots should be titrated immediately after degassing for 2 h with N2, and degassing should continue during the titration. CO2 removal methods that use heat (heating or refluxing) are inadequate to eliminate the effect of dissolved CO2, resulting in extremely broad variations. These techniques should not be used to remove CO2. Based on this research, degassing the solutions for 2 h with the N2 gas, followed by continued degasification of the solutions during the titration, is recommended for CO2 removal in the standardized Boehm titration [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of AC samples:
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 shows the XRD spectra of AC and functionalized samples (OAC-HN, OAC-Am, and OAC-OH). The broad asymmetric peak centered at 2θ ~ 23.8° (see Fig. S2) indicates the decreases in lattice spacing around (002) diffraction plane of graphite (ideally 2θ = 26.4°). This XRD peaks suggests the formation of sp2 hybridized crystalline carbon surrounded by sp3 hybridized amorphous carbon, indicating a non-perfect graphitic structure in CNPs [57]. Fig. S2 has a weak diffraction peak (2θ = 42.8°), corresponding to (101) plane, which can be attributed to the a-axis of the graphite structure [58,59]. Additionally, the pattern showed peaks at ~ 30°, 35.5°, and 41° which correspond to the (400), (311), and (107) graphite diffraction planes, respectively (JCPDS File, No. 1–640) [60]. Our XRD patterns indicated a typical amorphous carbon shape and exhibited broad asymmetric peaks consistent with the results found in previous studies by Kien Tiek Wong et al.,(2015) and Gaurav Kumar Yogesh et al., (2019) [57,60]. Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of as-prepared AC and OAC samples. The images reveal that AC and OAC have a narrow size distribution and are well separated from each other with a spherical morphology in aqueous media. The average particle size of AC was found to be 4.4 ± 0.5 nm. After oxidation, the average particle size was found to decrease to 3.9 ± 0.3 nm (OAC-OH), 3.13 ± 0.2 nm (OAC-Am), and 2.7 ± 0.3 nm (OAC-HN), as observed by TEM (see Fig. 2f-h). Gaurav et al. (2017) synthesized CNPs from AC. They found that the average particle size of CNPs are around 4 nm, which is similar to the size of our particles [61].

The decrease in the particle size may be attributed to the etching process of the carbon surface that happened during the acid treatment [62]. On the other hand, Fig. 2i-l shows the presence of crystalline lattice in the particles. The average lattice spacing is found to be between 0.27 ± 0.01 nm and 0.31 ± 0.01 nm, which is close to a spacing of (002) facets of graphite (0.34 nm) [63].

Boehm titration method was used to quantify various functional groups on the surfaces of the AC samples using selective neutralization capacity [54]. Fig. 3 a shows the values of surface functionality (SF; meq/g) of the samples (AC, OAC-HN, OAC-Am, and OAC-OH). The results indicate that the AC sample is mainly decorated by the carbonyl group due to activation treatment. The treatment of the AC sample with nitric acid, ammonium persulfate, and hydrogen peroxide has reduced the carbonyl group's density. Phenol, lactone, and carboxyl groups replaced the carbonyl groups. This is because the acidic treatment leads to oxidation of carbonyl groups. The sample of OAC-HN contains the highest amount of phenol, lactone, and carboxyl groups. It is known that, treatment of particles with low concentration of nitric acid leads to the formation of nitrogen based functional groups on the carbon surface. However, treatment with concentrated nitric acid (which is the case in this study) leads to the formation of carboxylic groups [64,65]. This could be the reason why we did not observe any nitrogen based groups on the surface of OAC-HN. In general, it can be observed that (Fig. 3a) the percentage of carbonyl group density is decreased by 80 % meq/g, and the carboxylic group density significantly increased by 350 % meq/g after surface oxidation.

The point of zero charges (pHzpc) of AC is 8.3, whereas for the modified AC samples like OAC-HN, it showed a pHzpc value at 2.7, indicating higher surface acidity than OAC-Am (4.1) and OAC-OH (6.7) (see Fig. 3b). This increase in surface acidity is attributed to the increased density of carboxylic groups due to oxidation [66,67].

Fig. 3c shows the zeta potential (δ) values of the AC and OAC samples as a function of pH. As the pH of the solution increases, δ changes from positive to negative values. The negative surface charge of the particles can be attributed to the presence of deprotonated hydroxyl ions of the carboxylic group which are present on the surface of NPs. Harry et al. have shown that the deprotonation of hydroxyl ions mostly occurs in the pH range 2–6 [50]. This is evident in our results as well. The AC sample shows decreasing positive values of δ up to pH 3.6 and thereafter it changes to negative. The pH at which δ = 0 shifts from 5.50 (OAC-OH) to 3.59 (OAC-HN). This is clearly because of the increasing density of carboxylic group on the surface of the OAC samples.

FT-IR results show the presence of different oxygen-based functional groups (see Fig. 3d). The broadband between 3400 and 3250 cm−1 is generally assigned to the OH stretching vibrational mode. Fig. 3d also shows the spectra of the OAC samples (OAC-HN, OAC-Am, and OAC-OH). The spectra show a band at 1703 cm−1, suggesting an increase in the density of carboxylic groups on the surface of the OAC samples compared to the AC sample. A similar trend has been observed in the bands 1573 and 1160 cm−1, which can be assigned to C = C aromatic stretching vibration in all samples and C–O stretching vibration in the carbonyl, laconic, phenolic, and carboxylic acid groups. The treatment of the AC sample by nitric acid showed a higher degree of surface modification compared to the treatment by ammonium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide [68]. Azharul Islam et al. (2017) prepared activated carbon from Rattan stalks using NaOH activation. FTIR characterization showed the presence of oxygen functional groups (carboxylic, hydroxyl, and carbonyl) in the AC structure, which is in line with our results [69].

Table 1 and Fig. S3 shows the CHN and EDS results of the AC samples. It is found that AC has the highest carbon content and after oxidation, the percentage of carbon decreases. The oxygen content of the OAC samples is higher than AC. As expected, OAC has higher H/C and O/C ratios compared to AC. The O/C of OAC ratio increases from 0.17 (AC) to 0.55 (OAC-HN), a change of 69.1%. In contrast, the H/C ratio of OAC increased around 82.9% (from 0.013(AC) to 0.076 (OAC-HN)). The increase in the H/C ratio can be attributed to the gaining of a CH3 group, and the decrease in the O/C ratio is due to carboxylation [70]. E.I. El-Shafey et al. (2016) prepared activated carbon from date palm leaflets. The EDX, CHN, and FTIR results showed that the oxygen on the surface increased after oxidation, similar to our results [48].

The TGA-DSC measurements were carried out to check the thermal stability and thermal decomposition of the samples. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . The TGA measurements were taken while heating the sample at a rate of 20 °C/min under N2 atmosphere to 800 °C from room temperature. All samples show two temperature regions for the mass loss. The first range is from 20 to 100 °C and the second is from 100 to 800 °C. The first range of mass loss could be due to the loss of solvents and water by evaporation. The loss is found to be 6.4% (AC), 22.2% (OAC-OH), 43.2%(OAC-Am), and 75.3% (OAC-HN). The higher moisture content in the OAC samples, compared to AC, could be due to H-bonding between the H2O molecules and the abundant carbon–oxygen (C-O) species on OAC. The second region of mass loss between 100 and 800 °C is attributed to a gradual loss of oxygen-containing groups [71,72], and generation of CO2/CO gases [73,74]. The decomposition of the carboxylic, lactone, and anhydrides groups may result in the generation of CO2 /CO gases [73,75]. The significant weight loss in the OAC samples, compared to AC, suggests the presence of a high amount of carbon–oxygen groups on the OAC NPs. The DSC curves (Fig. 4b) of AC and OAC show one intense endothermic process starting at 15 °C and ending at 140 °C. This is due to the loss of water molecules and consistent with our TG results. The next endothermic peaks appeared around 665 °C. This is attributed to the decomposition of functional groups and is consistent with the TG results [76].

The elemental analysis and determination of surface composition were done by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra of the AC and OAC samples are shown in Fig. S4, and the results are tabulated in Table 2 . The two peaks, which were observed at the binding energies, 284 eV and 532 eV, can be attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively [77]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of as-prepared AC and OAC are shown in Fig. 5 a–d. It can be seen that C 1s spectra of OAC–Am and OAC–HN could be deconvoluted into five individual component peaks at 284.1 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, 286.6, and 287.8 eV. These peaks can be attributed to C-sp2, C = C, C–O, C = O, and O–C = O bonds, respectively [37,78]. While the C 1s spectrum of OAC–OH could be deconvoluted into four individual component peaks corresponding to C-sp2 (284 eV), C–C (285 eV), C–O (286 eV), and O–C = O (287.4 eV), indicating the existence of oxygen-containing groups in OAC. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 5a) of AC also indicates the presence of two peaks at 284.1 eV and 285 eV, which are attributed to C-sp2, C = C, respectively. Furthermore, high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 5e-h) indicate two characteristic peaks to C–O at 531.6 eV and O–C = O at 532.5 eV [37,78,79]. The results of XPS is consistent with the rest of our characterization, indicating the presence of oxygen based functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxylic, phenol and lactone.

The normalized absorption spectra of the AC and OAC nanoparticles dispersed in ethanol (The samples were sonicated prior to the experiment to ensure the complete dispersion of the particles in ethanol) are presented in Fig. 6 . The sharp peak at 221 nm and a shoulder at 262 nm are due to the π- π* transition of the C = C (corresponding to the core of the NPs) and n- π* transition of the C = O (corresponding to oxygenated functional groups of the NPs), respectively [80]. Spectra are normalized at 221 nm for a comparison of the relative absorption contribution by different functional groups. The OAC-OH sample shows highest OD at 262 nm and the AC sample shows the lowest OD. The other two samples (OAC-HN and OAC-Am) have absorbance between those of AC and OAC-OH at 262 nm. Gaurav et al. (2017) were synthesized the carbon nanoparticles from activated carbon. They found that absorption peak in the range 250 to 365 nm, corresponding to our result [61].

We correlate the relative change in OD with the competition between the surface functionality of various functional groups. A plausible explanation is given below. From the Boehm titration results (Section 3.2) we know that the total surface functionality (SF; meq/g) is increasing in the order 2.05, 2.24, 2.56 and 3.67 for AC, OAC-OH, OAC-Am and OAC-HN, respectively. However, in the same order of the samples, SF of the carbonyl group is decreasing (55 to 5.5%) whereas phenol group (containing hydroxyl group) is increasing (15 to 28%). Therefore, these two changes are in competition with the increasing SF of the carboxylic (20 to 50%) and lactone (10 to 16%) groups. This could be the reason why OAC-OH has a high OD compared to OAC-Am and OAC-HN.

The excitation spectrum shows that the emission intensity is maximum while exciting at 350 nm (see Fig. S5). Fig. 7 a displays the room temperature emission spectra (λexc = 350 nm) of the AC and OAC samples in ethanol having the same concentration (8x10-3 g/mL). The total emission intensity from the samples is found to increase in the order AC, OAC-OH, OAC-Am and OAC-HN respectively. We can correlate these results with the variation of SF discussed in Section 3.2. We have seen that SF of carbonyl group is decreasing (55 to 5.5%) and other groups such as phenol (15 to 28%), carboxyl (20 to 50%) and lactone (10 to 16%) are increasing in the order AC, OAC-OH, OAC-Am and OAC-HN. For the subsequent discussions, we consider this particular order of the samples.

For a detailed analysis, the emission spectra were deconvoluted with minimum number of sub peaks to attain the best fit (R2 ≈ 0.9999). The deconvoluted spectra of the AC and OAC samples are shown in Fig. 7b-e. The spectra of AC and OAC-OH were deconvoluted in to four sub peaks, which are labelled as Peak 1 (394 nm or 3.1 eV), Peak 2 (420 nm or 2.9 eV) Peak 3 (444 nm or 2.7 eV) and Peak 4 (483 nm or 2.5 eV). OAC-Am and OAC-HN showed a fifth peak (in addition to the four peaks observed in AC and OAC) at the low energy part of the emission spectrum named as Peak 5 (554 nm or 2.2 eV). Fig. 7f shows the integrated intensity as the percentage area of each sub peak of the samples. The integrated intensity of Peak 1 and Peak 3 decreases, whereas it increases for Peak 2, Peak 4 and Peak 5. The decreasing, highest energy peak (which is Peak 1 with energy 3.1 eV) may not be associated with carbonyl groups. This is because the HOMO-LUMO gap of the carbonyl groups on the surface of carbon dots are shown to be around 2.5 eV [28]. Therefore, Peak 3 (2.7 eV) can be attributed to emission involving the carbonyl groups. Fang et al. reported that isolated sp2 clusters on the surface of NPs could have HOMO-LUMO gap similar to pyridine derivative units, which is around 3 eV [27,28]. They have also shown that the gap originating from carbon core with size around 5 nm is negligible. The origin of peak 1 in our results could be associated with isolated sp2 clusters on the surface of the NPs. Another reason for not considering core related emission is that, we did not observe any blue shift in the emission peak as the particle size reduces from 4.4 ± 0.5 nm (AC) to 2.2 ± 0.3 nm (OAC-HN). Peak 2 (2.9 eV), which shows 12 to 48% change in the integrated intensity could be coming from the carboxylic functional groups. This is because, the carboxylic groups show the same trend in the variation of SF and have higher values of SF compared to the phenol and lactone groups. The main excitation could be to the unoccupied levels of isolated sp2 clusters and non-radiative transitions from this levels could populate the states created by other functional groups and defects. As SF of carboxylic group’s increases, the non-radiative transitions from high energy levels can increase and could be the reason for the reduction of Peak 1 intensity. Peak 4 (2.5 eV) and Peak 5 (2.2 eV) could be associated with the phenol and lactone groups, respectively. A theoretical calculation of the HOMO-LUMO gap of the phenol groups on graphene shows around 1.9 eV [81]. The SF of the lactone group is the least among the functional groups in our samples. From the analysis, Peak 5 appears only in the case of OAC-Am and OAC-HN where the SF of lactone reaches its highest value of 16% among the four samples. Whereas the SF of phenol group varies from 15 to 28% and should have a prominent contribution to emission compared to lactone groups. These are the reasons for assigning the contribution of Peak 4 and Peak 5 to the phenol and lactone groups, respectively. Fig. 8 shows a schematic presentation for the possible excitation and emission pathways from different states associated with various oxygen based functional groups on the surface of AC and OAC-HN (sample with highest SF). With 350 nm excitation, population of isolated sp2 clusters initiates the emission in AC. As the SF of the sample increases, the density of midlevel states associated with functional groups also increases. Subsequent enhancement of nonradiative transitions to these states decreases the emission intensity of Peak1. Interestingly transitions from these functional groups to the ground state become more radiative, which is reflected as an overall increase of the emission intensity of OAC-HN sample. The emission peaks matching the process involving different functional groups are also marked in Fig. 8.

The emission lifetime of AC and OAC samples were measured by exciting at 310 nm and Global Lifetime software was used for fitting the decay transients (Fig. 9 a and Fig. S6). All the samples show a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2. Fig. 9b shows the variation of the average lifetime (amplitude weighted) of the samples which is increasing (1.1 to 2.2 ns) in the same order of the samples as discussed earlier. The amplitude corresponding to τ1 is decreasing while the other amplitude corresponding to τ2 is increasing (Fig. 9c). The increasing amplitude of the long-lived component represents the overall rise in the radiative transitions from midlevel surface states created by functional groups. This result is also consistent with the model discussed earlier. Therefore it can be concluded that oxygen based functional groups on the surface of CNPs play an important role in the emission process and tunability of emission characteristics (spectrum, quantum yield) can be achieved by controlling the surface functionality of the desired groups.

Activated Carbon (AC) nanoparticles were successfully prepared from the green waste of mandarin peel. The surface modification was performed on the AC samples to achieve various levels of functionalization. The structural characterization reveals average lattice spacing of 0.31 ± 0.01 nm, which is close to (002) facets of graphite (0.34 nm). The average particle size of the samples is between 2.7 and 4.4 nm. The quantification of surface functional groups revealed a systematic variation of the amount of groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, phenol and lactone) that depends on the surface treatment carried out on the samples. Optical investigations show a clear dependence of the absorption and emission properties on the surface functional groups. Detailed analysis of the deconvoluted emission spectra yield a relationship between different emission pathways and specific functional groups on the surface of AC. Our results suggest that the energy gap of isolated sp2 cluster on the surface of NPs is the highest among the gaps related to various functional groups. The non-radiative transitions from this level can populate the mid emissive levels. As a proof for this model, the integrated intensity of the high-energy peak was found to decrease and a corresponding enhancement of low energy peaks was observed as the surface functionality of carboxyl groups increased. Our results suggests that various emissive pathways in CNPs can be controlled by selective surface functionalization. These results are quite substantial for the understanding of emission mechanism of carbon nanoparticles.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Sultan Qaboos University within the framework of research Internal Grant [IG/SCI/PHYS/18/05] and His Majesty’s Trust Fund for Strategic Research (SR/SCI/CHEM/18/01). Further, we thank the central analytical and applied research unit at Sultan Qaboos University for the XRD measurements. Mr. Abdul Rahman Al Nabhani, Department of pathology, Sultan Qaboos University is acknowledged for the TEM measurements and Prof. Salim Al-Harthi for XPS measurements.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mesoporous activated carbon prepared from NaOH activation of rattan (Lacosperma secundiflorum) hydrochar for methylene blue removal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Islam, M.A., et al., Mesoporous activated carbon prepared from NaOH activation of rattan (Lacosperma secundiflorum) hydrochar for methylene blue removal. Ecotoxicol Environ Saf, 2017. 138: p. 279-285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical modification of biomass residues during hydrothermal carbonization – What makes the difference, temperature or feedstock?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wiedner, K., et al., Chemical modification of biomass residues during hydrothermal carbonization – What makes the difference, temperature or feedstock? Organic Geochemistry, 2013. 54: p. 91-100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cysteamine hydrochloride protected carbon dots as a vehicle for the efficient release of the anti-schizophrenic drug haloperidol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pandey, S., et al., Cysteamine hydrochloride protected carbon dots as a vehicle for the efficient release of the anti-schizophrenic drug haloperidol. RSC Advances, 2013. 3(48): p. 26290.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and scalable preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for multifunctional applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, D., et al., Facile and Scalable Preparation of Fluorescent Carbon Dots for Multifunctional Applications. Engineering, 2017. 3(3): p. 402-408.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.L. Figueiredo, M.F.R.P., M.M.A. Freitas, J.J.M. Orfao, Modification of the surface chemistry of activated carbons, Carbon, 1999. 37: p. 1379-1389.

Y. Otake, R.G.J., Characterization of oxygen-containing surface complexes created on a microporous carbon by air and nitric acid treatment, Carbon, 1993. 31: p. 109-121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A water-stable La-MOF with high fluorescence sensing and supercapacitive performances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, Q.Q., et al., A water-stable La-MOF with high fluorescence sensing and supercapacitive performances. Analyst, 2019. 144(15): p. 4534-4544.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from natural materials of mango fruit for bio-imaging probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jeong, C.J., et al., Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from natural materials of mango fruit for bio-imaging probes. Nanoscale, 2014. 6(24): p. 15196-202.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yogesh, G.K., et al., Synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) from nanosecond pulsed laser ablation in ethanol. Optics & Laser Technology, 2021. 135: p. 106717.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lesiak, B., et al., C sp2/sp3 hybridisations in carbon nanomaterials – XPS and (X)AES study. Applied Surface Science, 2018. 452: p. 223-231.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chutia, A., et al., Influence of surface chemistry on the electronic properties of graphene nanoflakes. Chemical Physics Letters, 2011. 503(1-3): p. 91-96.
